title: Redirect Semantics, Method Rewriting, Credential Scope, and Loop Control · Browser and Node Client Systems · Type Forensics
summary: A browser support engineer tracing a cross-runtime client incident must isolate a plausible but incorrect result from conflicting traces, repair its cause, and retain a regression witness for redirect semantics, method rewriting, credential scope, and loop control, producing auditable redirect policy engine. Planned artifact: Repair a changed failure in a TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch auditable redirect policy engine and publish the reproduction, evidence, repair, and regression record. Le…
objectives: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Resolve Location references against the current request URL and validate the resulting destination each hop. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Bound redirect hops, detect loops, retain the chain, and expose the policy decision to callers. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Inspect the failure record · Debug Redirect Status Semantics
steps.instruction:
debug case — cross-runtime browser support client sends POST T-5 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

Inspect the Redirect Status Semantics trace for this debug case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Inspect the failure record · Debug Redirect Status Semantics.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for differentiate 301, 302, 303, 307, and 308 cache, permanence, method, and body consequences. demonstrate the result through typescript 7 strict types plus whatwg fetch request, response, headers, url, streams, and abortsignal contracts. For Fetch and TypeScript Inspect the failure record · Debug Redirect Status Semantics, keep Redirect Status Semantics connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Inspect the failure record · Debug Redirect Status Semantics, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: All redirect status codes preserve the original method and body. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

steps.title: Fetch and TypeScript Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Manual Redirect Defense
steps.instruction:
Add a typed evidence_http_ts_manual_redirect_defense_18xfd6 function that uses deterministic TypeScript evidence to decide redirect following, method preservation, hop limits, and credential forwarding independently.

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Manual Redirect Defense.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Manual Redirect Defense into observable performance evidence. For Fetch and TypeScript Implement the cause-level repair · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, keep Manual Redirect Defense connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.

This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.

Evidence target: Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense.
steps.why: Retrieval and explanation expose gaps that recognition questions can hide. For Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, keep Manual Redirect Defense connected to executable HTTP client decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Choose automatic, manual, or rejected redirects from method replay, SSRF, credential, audit, and user-consent risk. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

hints: For Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, name the method, authority, body, byte, time, and credential boundary, Manual Redirect Defense precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, trace this concrete input and predict resolution, redirect, status, representation, retry, and cleanup evidence before running it: cross-runtime browser support client sends POST T-3 with authorization, then receives 302 ../moved, 307 https://upload.example.test/next, and a loop back to the first URL. Predict method, body, credential, origin, hop-budget, and SSRF decisions per step. Competency probe: Choose automatic, manual, or rejected redirects from method replay, SSRF, credential, audit, and user-consent risk. Demonstrate the result through TypeScript 7 strict types plus WHATWG Fetch Request, Response, Headers, URL, streams, and AbortSignal contracts.

For Fetch and TypeScript Publish the incident record · Debug Manual Redirect Defense, challenge the documented misconception with request, response, fault, policy, and transfer evidence: Following redirects is transport plumbing with no application or security semantics. This remains false for TypeScript 7 standards-based Fetch.
